prompt: How much does food quality really matter for strength and hypertrophy if calories and protein are already decent? Am I losing meaningful gains by eating more fun foods?
expected_output: Their answer was that it depends, with big modifiers: overall health status, age/body composition, and whether your diet creates real nutrient deficiencies. Greg said if someone is young, generally healthy, active, meeting calories/protein, and not deficient, the direct strength/hypertrophy penalty from imperfect food quality is probably small. He thought improving diet quality in that case may matter more for feeling better and long-term health than for huge short-term gains differences. They both emphasized that many concerns are about missing beneficial foods/nutrients rather than the mere presence of enjoyable calorie-dense foods. They also noted context where less nutrient-dense additions can be useful, like helping athletes meet high energy demands. Practical takeaway: secure essentials (energy, protein, micronutrient adequacy), then treat food-quality upgrades as an important but context-dependent performance and health lever.
assertions: Answer states the direct strength/hypertrophy penalty from imperfect food quality is probably small if basics are met
Answer notes many concerns are about missing beneficial foods, not the mere presence of fun foods
Answer recommends securing essentials (energy, protein, micronutrient adequacy) first
Answer frames food-quality upgrades as context-dependent health and performance lever
